L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and was 'desperate to get one final jump in before his flight home the next morning.' This time pressure and desperation to complete a final jump drove him to proceed despite conditions that two companions judged unjumpable and despite being directly told it was not worth it.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'seemed in a rush because he wanted to go to sleep early to catch the early morning conditions.' This haste during packing directly contributed to the bridle misrouting error by reducing the care and attention applied to the rigging process.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his partner performed a pre-exit gear check despite standing right next to each other at the exit point. The narrative explicitly states this omission, indicating a failure to maintain sufficient vigilance over a routine safety procedure that would have immediately detected the misrouted bridle.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration for the planned jump
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed the evening before the jump at the campground while in a rush, and then geared up in the dark by headlamp on a ledge below the exit. Packing in haste and gearing up in low-visibility conditions created the environment in which the bridle misrouting occurred and went undetected.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Flat light conditions over a featureless snowfield with heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, and no texture eliminated all visual depth cues. This caused the jumper to misperceive his altitude and distance from terrain, directly producing the altitude awareness failure identified at L1.

L2_preconditions.label: Flat light over featureless snowfield
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture on the snowfield created complete flat light conditions that physically eliminated the visual depth cues necessary for altitude perception during wingsuit flight. This environmental condition directly degraded the jumper's ability to perceive his proximity to terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind gust on exit
L2_preconditions.description: The 20-knot sustained crosswind with gusts over 30 knots caught the jumper immediately on exit, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall and destabilizing him from stable to head-low within the first second. This environmental force directly degraded his ability to maintain a stable exit and reach his pilot chute, contributing to the failed exit and no-deployment outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to remain at the exit and wait after four experienced companions all stood down, then launched into conditions they unanimously judged unsafe. This behavior indicates a misplaced motivation to complete the jump that overrode the clear social and environmental signals to stand down, contributing to the decision to jump in dangerous winds.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was severely hungover after drinking heavily until 4am the night before. The narrative explicitly states he was visibly unsteady, had a splitting headache, and vomited twice on the hike. The narrative directly attributes his impaired coordination on exit to the hangover, which caused his legs to buckle on push-off producing the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper fixated entirely on recovering a stable body position to the exclusion of the critical task of deploying his pilot chute. The narrative explicitly states he spent the entire freefall trying to get back to belly rather than pitching his PC, channeling all attention on stability recovery while ignoring the higher-priority deployment task.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The severe hangover degraded the jumper's cognitive capacity, plausibly impairing his ability to prioritize deployment over stability recovery during the high-workload freefall situation. The narrative establishes he was clearly still impaired at the time of the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper had jumped hungover before without issues, indicating a normalized pattern of jumping while impaired by alcohol aftereffects. This sustained acceptance of jumping in a degraded physical state was a known recurring deficiency he failed to correct, which enabled the impaired exit on this occasion.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and had never jumped it from a cliff. Accepted practice requires a minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE jumping with a tracking suit, and mastery of the ideal body position for maximum forward speed and glide. This massive training deficit directly enabled his inability to achieve forward drive during the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue from overnight drive
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit. Physical fatigue from sleep deprivation degrades coordination and motor performance, which would have impaired his ability to execute the precise body configuration required to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from video viewing
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper declined a suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen intermediate-to-advanced exit 'looked easy from the footage.' With only two tracking suit skydives and zero cliff tracking suit jumps, his assessment that the exit looked easy was an unreasonable overestimation of his capability, driven by watching videos rather than actual experience.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient off-heading correction training
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper's reaction to the 180° off-heading—going directly for toggles instead of rear risers—suggests insufficient trained muscle memory for the accepted rear-riser correction technique. Accepted practice emphasizes extensive practice of instant heading corrections at simple spans so that the correct response (rear risers first) becomes automatic. The procedural error is consistent with a capability gap that more deliberate off-heading correction training would have closed.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated he had done this exit dozens of times and knew the heading by feel, believing he could safely navigate by feel alone in zero visibility. This unreasonable overestimation of his ability to track a safe line without visual reference — when all other jumpers recognized the conditions as unjumpable and packed up to hike down — directly enabled his decision to exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's attention was channelized on the camera adjustment task, consuming his cognitive focus to the exclusion of monitoring terrain proximity and maintaining situational awareness. This fixation on the camera directly enabled his failure to perceive the approaching ledge and make corrective inputs despite it being in clear view for several seconds.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The helmet-mounted GoPro camera equipment created an external stimulus that diverted the jumper's attention from the primary task of tracking away from terrain. The presence of camera equipment and an apparent issue with it prompted the decision to adjust it mid-flight rather than maintain focus on the tracking task.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls over the preceding three months, explicitly acknowledged his AoA control was 'sloppy,' stated he should return to skydiving for 50 dedicated stall-recovery practice jumps, and never did so. He continued jumping cliffs every weekend without addressing the known recurring deficiency, normalizing a pattern of near-miss stalls that directly enabled the fatal stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses stated the jumper 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him,' with his arms remaining at his sides and no corrective input attempted. This automatic physiological freeze response triggered by the unexpected sight of guy wires directly ahead rendered him temporarily unable to act, directly enabling the failure to correct the off-heading.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient antenna experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two previous antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. With only 60 total BASE jumps and minimal antenna-specific experience, he lacked the practiced muscle memory for instant off-heading corrections in the confined heading environment of a guyed antenna. Accepted practice emphasizes that heading correction skills must be practiced extensively at forgiving objects so that reactions become automatic — a capability gap that directly contributed to his inability to act when confronted with the off-heading toward guy wires.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper overestimated his ability to assess the damaged PC's airworthiness, dismissing advice from multiple other jumpers who told him it needed replacement and offered spares. His confidence in his own assessment ('it would still catch enough air') overrode the consensus judgment of his peers, directly enabling his decision to jump with the damaged PC.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way briefing and separation plan
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'Nobody really went through the exit order or separation plan properly' and 'No specific delays were set and no one talked about flight paths or opening altitudes.' Accepted practice for multi-way BASE jumps requires thorough planning of exit order, delays, flight paths, and staged openings with vertical and horizontal separation. This failure to establish a separation plan created the conditions under which David tracked directly over the second jumper and deployed into his canopy without adequate spatial awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention to airspace
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'David did not check his airspace before deploying,' indicating a failure to maintain situational awareness of other jumpers' positions during a multi-way jump. This failure of attention management—not scanning for the second jumper who was in close proximity—directly enabled the deployment into the inflating canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been warned by multiple other jumpers about his progressively longer delays over the past season. Despite this feedback, he failed to correct the behavior, continuing to push delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds. This pattern of ignoring repeated warnings and normalizing increasingly risky delay times directly enabled the fatal decision to pull too low on this jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience in expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit before taking it off a cliff. The domain reference states that even an entry-level suit requires 30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential, and that upgrading wingsuits before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error. The narrative explicitly states that an expert-level wingsuit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand, and he had not put in that time. This insufficient familiarity with the suit's stall characteristics directly enabled the AoA management failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper's angle of attack shifting mid-flight and his failure to recognize and correct the developing stall over approximately two seconds. This suggests a failure in airspeed awareness and situational monitoring — he did not detect the degrading flight state in time to take corrective action, consistent with channelized attention on other aspects of the flight (e.g., the line or terrain) to the exclusion of monitoring speed and AoA.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down due to deteriorating weather, strong winds, and rain. This deliberation over the go/no-go decision likely diverted cognitive attention away from the packing procedure, contributing to the critical error of placing the pilot chute inside the container instead of routing it to the BOC pouch.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check verifying that the pilot chute was properly routed to the BOC pouch would have detected the packing error before exit. The narrative provides no indication that such a check was performed, and the error—PC packed inside the container rather than in the BOC—is the type of gross rigging mistake that a systematic equipment inspection would have caught.
